title: Rail Artifact Identity Contract
description: Example fixed-format ContextKit spec for Rail artifact identity and projection authority.
humanSummary: Rail work is identified by artifact handles, not request files or inferred task names. Users should be able to resume or inspect work through durable artifacts.
agentSummary: When changing artifact identity, update runtime contracts, tests, and any skill surface that describes start, resume, retry, status, or result behavior.
goal: Show how a durable runtime contract can name identity rules, owned surfaces, validation, and agent instructions in one structured JSON document.
architecture: Rail separates request drafting, artifact persistence, supervision, and result projection. Artifact identity belongs to the persisted artifact layer, while supervision and result projection consume validated handles.
nonGoals: This example does not replace Rail's canonical docs.
This example does not define new runtime behavior.
evidence: Existing Rail docs distinguish product spec, architecture, and conventions.
Runtime API examples expose start_task, supervise, and result around durable handles.
validationCommands: uv run --python 3.12 ruff check src tests
agentInstructions: Read this spec before changing artifact identity.
Update linked skills and tests when public artifact behavior changes.
reviewChecklist: The source of truth is explicit and current.
Contract rules are testable or tied to validation evidence.
problem: Artifact identity rules are easy to blur when plans mention request files, target-local state, or generated output. That can make agents infer the wrong resume or projection authority.

vocabulary.meaning: Durable identity returned by Rail for task or goal work.
Status or result read from persisted artifacts.

contract: Fresh work creates a fresh artifact handle.
Existing work resumes only from a validated handle.
invariants: Request files are not durable run identity.
Terminal success comes from gated artifacts.
